mnemonics.title: AI ETHICS PRINCIPLES
mnemonics.content: TRAIN: TRANSPARENCY (explainable), RESPONSIBILITY (accountability), AUTONOMY (human oversight), INCLUSIVITY (fairness), NON-MALEFICENCE (do no harm)

mnemonics.title: AI RISK CATEGORIES
mnemonics.content: SECRET: SOCIAL (job loss, inequality), ECONOMIC (bias, fraud), CIVIL (surveillance, privacy), ENVIRONMENTAL (energy), REGULATORY (compliance), CULTURAL (deepfakes)

mnemonics.title: RESPONSIBLE AI STEPS
mnemonics.content: AUDIT: ASSESS (impact), UNDERSTAND (data), DOCUMENT (decisions), TEST (fairness), UPDATE (continuously)

acronyms.expansion: Explainable AI - AI systems that provide understandable explanations for their decisions
acronyms.usage: Used in healthcare and finance where decision transparency is legally required

acronyms.expansion: Human-in-the-Loop - Design approach where humans oversee AI decisions
acronyms.usage: Critical for AI systems making life-altering decisions like loan approvals or medical diagnoses

acronyms.expansion: General Data Protection Regulation - EU law governing data privacy
acronyms.usage: Applies to AI systems processing personal data of EU residents

acronyms.expansion: Generative Adversarial Networks - AI technique for creating synthetic content
acronyms.usage: Used to create deepfakes and synthetic media, raising ethical concerns

acronyms.expansion: National Institute of Standards and Technology - US agency developing AI risk frameworks
acronyms.usage: Provides guidelines for trustworthy AI development and testing

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a pyramid: BOTTOM (minimal risk - recommendation systems), MIDDLE (limited risk - chatbots), TOP (high risk - hiring AI), PEAK (unacceptable - social scoring)
Think of a balance scale: on one side protected groups, on the other side equal treatment. Tilt either way indicates bias.
Picture an onion with layers: CORE (data minimization), MIDDLE (encryption), OUTER (access controls). Each layer protects privacy.

analogies.analogy: Like a black box on an airplane - you know inputs go in and outputs come out, but cannot see inside to understand how decisions are made. This makes debugging and accountability difficult.
Like teaching a child using only one textbook written by people with the same viewpoint - the child develops a limited, potentially skewed understanding of the world.
Like a movie rating board - diverse experts review AI 'films' before public release to ensure they don't harm audiences and meet societal standards.
Like a hotel room - you should only take what you need for your stay, not fill your suitcase with everything available. AI should use minimal data for its purpose.
